Spec referenced: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-17-tests-as-context-design.md

title: TS test-DSL: support describes inside for/forEach loops
description: Current test-scope-chunker only walks direct namedChildren of statement_block. Dynamic describes wrapped in for/forEach/map loops (e.g. CONFIG_MAPPINGS pattern in tests/bootstrap/ingest-env.test.ts) are absorbed into parent scope's otherLines and emitted as a single test_setup chunk instead of distinct test chunks per iteration. Ruby has the same limitation (each do |x| describe ... end). Fix: extend buildScopeTree to recursively walk through non-DSL statement bodies (for_statement.body, call_expression callbacks like forEach/map, etc.) looking for nested DSL calls. Affects both Ruby and TS hooks for parity. Tracked separately because v1 ships with the same limitation as Ruby — paritet preserved.

title: Migrate remaining 6 derived signals to unified confidence
description: Follow-up from epic tea-rags-mcp-vpn6. After Task 5 of plan docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-14-hotspot-signal-interpretation-impl.md, BugFixSignal is migrated. 6 signals still use dampeningSource = GIT_FILE_DAMPENING + FALLBACK_THRESHOLD pattern: VolatilitySignal (k=8), RecentActivityConcentrationSignal (k=5), DensitySignal (k=5), RelativeChurnNormSignal (k=5), KnowledgeSiloSignal (k=5), OwnershipSignal (k=5). Each needs its own stats.confidence.score block on the matching raw payload signal in payload-signals.ts. See .claude/rules/signal-confidence.md migration steps.
close_reason: All 6 remaining derived signals migrated at 654b8385. DampeningConfig + constants.ts deleted, dampeningSource field removed from interface. Adaptive label thresholds and adaptivePercentile in score declaration also implemented.

title: Publication artifacts: blog post + arXiv draft + HN submission
description:
Final publication phase of the benchmark portfolio (parent epic tea-rags-mcp-evpa).

Goal: convert raw numbers into industry visibility. Without this step, the benchmarks do not approve anything publicly.

Three artifacts:

1. Blog post — "Benchmarking git-aware code retrieval for AI agents"
   Target venues: tea-rags website + cross-post to Medium/Substack/personal blog
   Outline:
   - Why current code-retrieval benchmarks miss the point for agents
   - Three-layer benchmark portfolio (CoIR / SWE-bench / GitRankBench)
   - Headline result: tea-rags vs baselines across all three
   - Honest section: where tea-rags doesn't help (small bugs, simple lookups)
   - Reproducibility: link to Docker harness
   Length: 2500-4000 words, 5-10 charts
   Charts: NDCG@10 table, % resolved with CI, tool calls distribution, per-signal ablation

2. Hacker News submission
   Title: "Benchmarking code-retrieval quality for AI coding agents [tea-rags]"
   Submit Tuesday/Wednesday 9am ET (best engagement window historically)
   Have a one-paragraph TL;DR ready for first comment
   Prepare to answer technical questions for 4-6 hours after submission
   Cross-post to lobste.rs, r/LocalLLaMA, r/MachineLearning, dev.to

3. arXiv preprint — "GitRankBench: A Benchmark for Git-Aware Code Retrieval"
   Format: 6-8 page workshop paper
   Sections:
   - Introduction (why git-aware retrieval matters for agents)
   - Related work (CSN, CoIR, SWE-bench — what they miss)
   - Benchmark design (corpus, queries, labeling)
   - Baselines and method
   - Results with ablation
   - Limitations and future work
   Submit to: cs.IR + cs.SE categories
   Target venue (optional follow-up): ICSE, FSE, or RAG workshop at NeurIPS

Pre-publication checklist:
- All hypotheses pre-registered in dated Github issue BEFORE first run
- Confidence intervals on every reported number
- Discussion of limitations is at least 15% of body text
- Reproducibility kit linked from abstract
- Acknowledgments section for any contributor

Network outreach (parallel):
- Tag relevant people on X: Beyang Liu (Sourcegraph), Hamel Husain, Simon Willison, Eugene Yan, Logan Kilpatrick (Google AI), Sourcegraph DevRel
- DM follow-up to people who engage on HN/X
- Send to AI Engineer World's Fair / DevTools Summit CFP if dates align

Acceptance:
- Blog post published with URL
- HN submission has either >100 upvotes OR >25 comments (signal of engagement)
- arXiv preprint has a stable URL
- At least 3 substantive external mentions (X, blog, podcast)

Estimated effort: 2-3 weeks (writing + revision + outreach)

title: Reproducibility kit: Docker, scripts, raw outputs
description:
Cross-cutting infrastructure for the benchmark suite (parent epic tea-rags-mcp-evpa).

Goal: any third-party engineer must be able to run all three benchmarks (CoIR, SWE-bench Lite, GitRankBench) with a single docker run and reproduce the published numbers within statistical noise.

Components:

1. Single repository: tea-rags-bench (separate from tea-rags-mcp main repo)
   Structure:
     /coir/        — CoIR adapter + scripts
     /swebench/    — SWE-bench Lite harness
     /gitrank/     — GitRankBench eval
     /docker/      — Per-benchmark Dockerfiles
     /scripts/     — End-to-end run scripts
     /results/     — Raw outputs (gitignored, downloaded from releases)
     /docs/        — How to reproduce

2. Per-benchmark Docker images:
   - Pinned versions of all deps (tea-rags, Qdrant, embedding model, Claude SDK)
   - Pinned dataset snapshots (CoIR commit SHA, SWE-bench dataset version, GitRankBench frozen corpus)
   - Health-check scripts to verify environment

3. Single-command run interface:
   docker run tea-rags-bench:latest run coir --dataset=csn-ccr-python
   docker run tea-rags-bench:latest run swebench-lite --config=tea-rags-full
   docker run tea-rags-bench:latest run gitrank --category=bug-prone

4. Raw outputs as release artifacts:
   - predictions.json per configuration (qid -> ranked doc list)
   - metrics.json (aggregate + per-instance)
   - logs (truncated tool traces for top-50 most-interesting tasks)

5. Anti-contamination kit:
   - Pinned model versions (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Opus 4.6, etc.)
   - Date-stamped dataset cuts
   - Statement about what model cutoff vs benchmark date overlap

Acceptance:
- External tester can clone repo, install Docker, and reproduce headline numbers in under 4 hours
- All raw outputs available as Github Release assets
- README with quickstart + reproducibility statement

Estimated effort: 1-2 weeks (parallelizable with benchmark dev)

title: GitEnrichmentAblationBench: 4 real-world tasks proving git signals add value
description:
First public benchmark proving git enrichment contributes measurable value on top of any embedding model, across categories where git signals have semantic ground.

Core principle: A/B ablation with IDENTICAL embedding model (jina-v2-base-code) and IDENTICAL hybrid retrieval on both arms. Delta isolates the pure git contribution, decoupled from embedding choice.

Four experiments, each activates a specific tea-rags preset, each has natural ground truth from git/CVE (NO LLM-as-judge required, NO human labeling).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
EXP 1 — Bug debugging
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preset tested: bug-hunt (bugFixRate + churn + recency + similarity)
Corpus: SWE-bench Lite bug-fix subset (~200 tasks)
Ground truth: files modified by golden patch (FREE, from dataset)
ARM A: hybrid + structural rerank
ARM B: hybrid + bug-hunt preset
Metric: Target File Recall@1, Recall@5
Hypothesis: ARM B gives +10-20pp Recall@1 on tasks where filename is NOT mentioned in issue text

NOTE: this experiment OVERLAPS with tea-rags-mcp-pdpe (SWE-bench ablation).
      Coordinate to avoid duplicate runs. Either run once and report twice,
      or split: pdpe = SWE-bench focus, 35hr = the other 3 experiments.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
EXP 2 — Tech debt audit
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preset tested: techDebt (age + commitCount + churn)
Corpus: 5 OSS repos with 5+ year history, frozen snapshot
        (Django, Postgres, React, Rails, TypeScript compiler)
Ground truth (natural, from git itself):
  Primary:   files in top decile by (ageDays × commitCount) — by definition, legacy+active
  Secondary: files mentioned in commit messages containing
             ["deprecated", "legacy", "cleanup", "migrate from", "remove old"]
  Validation: spot-check 50 files manually that they look legitimately tech-debt
Queries: 30 generic "find legacy code in domain X" queries per repo
ARM A: hybrid without git
ARM B: techDebt preset with git
Metric: NDCG@10 against ground truth
Hypothesis: ARM B gives +30-50pp NDCG — because "tech debt" has NO semantic signature
            in code itself; retrieval without git signals is essentially random for this query type

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
EXP 3 — Knowledge silos
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preset tested: ownership / knowledgeSilo
Corpus: same 5 OSS repos
Ground truth (deterministic, computed from git blame at snapshot):
  Silo files: blameDominantAuthorPct ≥ 90% AND blameContributorCount ≤ 2
  Non-silo:   blameContributorCount ≥ 5 AND blameDominantAuthorPct ≤ 40%
Queries: 20 queries per repo like "modules owned by a single person",
         "code with single maintainer", "bus factor risks"
ARM A: hybrid without git, same queries
ARM B: ownership preset with git
Metric: Precision@10, Recall@20
Hypothesis: ARM A gives near-zero Precision — "owned by one person" has no semantic
            signature in code text. ARM B gives high Precision — directly ranks by ownership.
            This is the most dramatic delta — a category where hybrid retrieval is
            physically incapable, and git enrichment solves the problem entirely.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
EXP 4 — Security audit
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preset tested: securityAudit (pathRisk + age + similarity)
Corpus: OSS projects with public CVE history
        (Django, Flask, Rails, Spring, Express, axios)
Ground truth: from CVE database — patched files for each CVE
Setup per CVE:
  - Snapshot repo at commit BEFORE the patch
  - Query = CVE description text
  - Target = files modified by the security patch
ARM A: hybrid without git
ARM B: securityAudit preset
Metric: Target File Recall@10
Hypothesis: ARM B gives +15-25pp Recall@10 — pathRisk (auth/, security/, crypto/)
            + age (older code more likely to contain known CVE patterns) help locate
            CVE-affected files that don't textually match the description.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Cross-cutting requirements:
- Pre-registered hypotheses BEFORE first run (anti-cherry-picking)
- Bootstrap 95% CI on every reported number
- Wilcoxon signed-rank for paired comparisons
- Per-signal contribution ablation (drop one signal, measure delta)
- Stratification by repo size and language
- Open dataset + reproducibility kit (Docker)

What this REPLACES from the original GitRankBench plan:
- No LLM-as-judge (ground truth is from git/CVE directly)
- No human labeling for v0
- No 200-300 hand-written queries; smaller focused query sets per experiment
- No paper-track delay — this is the MAIN approve, not a future research artifact

Acceptance:
- Public repo with all 4 experiments' code + datasets + Docker
- 4 result tables with 95% CI, p-values, per-signal ablation
- Blog post-ready figures
- Reusable as ongoing regression test when changing presets

Estimated effort: 3-4 weeks for v0
Estimated API cost: $300-700 (no LLM-as-judge → much cheaper than original plan)

title: SWE-bench Lite ablation: pure git enrichment contribution to bug debugging
description:
Reframed: main metric is NOT % resolved. Main metric is Target File Recall@K in an A/B ablation with same embedding model.

Why reframed: % resolved is dominated by agent reasoning, not retrieval. Headline number would be small (1-5pp) and easy to dismiss. The right question is "does adding git signals to ranking help the agent find the right file faster?" — that's a clean retrieval-layer ablation, decoupled from agent skill.

Experimental design — A/B ablation, same embedding (jina-v2-base-code), same hybrid retrieval:

  ARM A (control):   tea-rags hybrid + structural rerank only       (NO git signals)
  ARM B (treatment): tea-rags hybrid + bug-hunt preset               (WITH git signals)

Both arms use Claude Sonnet via Claude Code SDK with tea-rags MCP attached. Index strictly at base_commit (no leak from golden patch history).

Corpus:
- SWE-bench Lite, 300 tasks across 12 repos
- Stratified: split tasks by "filename mentioned in issue" vs "filename NOT mentioned"
- Hypothesis: git signals help in NOT-mentioned subset (where retrieval is actually needed); near-zero effect in mentioned subset (where any retriever finds the right file trivially)

Primary metrics (per arm, per subset):
- Target File Recall@1, @5, @10 — was the file containing the golden patch in top-K of first search?
- Target Chunk Recall@5, @10 — was the specific code block touching the patch in top-K?
- Search Depth to Target — how many search queries before the agent reached target file?

Secondary metrics:
- Tool calls per task
- Input tokens per task
- % resolved (reported but NOT framed as headline)

Variance control:
- 3 independent runs per arm with different temperature seeds
- Wilcoxon signed-rank test on paired tasks (same task across both arms)
- Bootstrap 95% CI on Recall@K
- Pre-registered hypotheses in dated GitHub issue BEFORE first run

Anti-contamination:
- SWE-bench Live (last 3 months, ~50 tasks) as supplement
- Compare ARM B - ARM A delta between Lite and Live; if delta consistent → not driven by memorization

Per-signal contribution (optional ablation matrix):
- ARM B_no_bugfix: bug-hunt preset minus bugFixRate signal
- ARM B_no_churn: bug-hunt preset minus chunkChurnRatio signal
- ARM B_no_age: bug-hunt preset minus chunkAgeDays signal
Each variant shows individual signal's contribution to Recall@K.

Acceptance:
- Public repo with harness code + raw predictions per arm
- Honest discussion of where git signals help and where they don't
- Stratified breakdown (filename mentioned vs not, file size, repository)
- All raw outputs as Github Release artifacts

Estimated effort: 2-3 weeks
Estimated API cost: $200-400 (2 arms × 3 runs × 300 tasks × Claude Sonnet)

title: CoIR adapter + benchmark run on CodeSearchNet-CCR
description:
Layer A of the benchmark portfolio (parent epic tea-rags-mcp-evpa).

Goal: produce first public retrieval-quality numbers comparable to CoIR leaderboard.

Steps:
1. pip install coir-eval, clone CoIR-Retrieval datasets
2. Write TeaRagsRetriever class implementing CoIR retriever interface:
   - encode_corpus(corpus) -> writes docs to temp dir, calls index_codebase
   - search(queries, top_k=100) -> dispatches to hybrid_search or semantic_search via MCP/HTTP, maps file paths back to doc_ids
3. Run on CodeSearchNet-CCR-Python (~28K queries) in 4 modes:
   - jina-v2-code raw cosine (baseline, no tea-rags)
   - tea-rags semantic only (no rerank)
   - tea-rags hybrid (BM25 + dense + RRF)
   - tea-rags hybrid + structural rerank
4. Collect: NDCG@10, MRR@10, Recall@100, latency p50/p95
5. Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals (1000 resamples)
6. Compare to CoIR public leaderboard entries for jina-v2-code

Acceptance:
- Public repo with adapter code + Dockerfile
- Results table in README + raw predictions.json artifact
- 3 independent runs to verify variance < 1% NDCG

Warning: CoIR datasets have no git history → git signals do not activate. This layer measures basic hybrid+rerank quality only, not tea-rags differentiators.

Estimated effort: 1-2 weeks
Estimated API cost: minimal (CoIR is offline retrieval, no LLM in the loop)
close_reason: Superseded by ablation-first strategy. CoIR measures absolute retrieval quality (NDCG@10), which is dominated by the embedding model (jina-v2-base-code), not by tea-rags-specific git enrichment. Wrong question for our value proposition. Replaced by Eksp.1-4 in GitEnrichmentAblationBench (tea-rags-mcp-35hr) and refocused SWE-bench task (tea-rags-mcp-pdpe), both of which use A/B ablation with identical embedding model on both arms — isolating the pure git enrichment delta.

title: Benchmark suite for tea-rags: approve quality publicly
description:
Goal: prove that git enrichment adds measurable value on top of any embedding model, in real-world task categories where git signals have semantic ground. NOT to prove absolute retrieval quality (that's dominated by embedding choice, not by tea-rags).

Reframed 2026-05-13 after a key insight:
  Original plan included CoIR retrieval-quality baseline. This was WRONG because
  CoIR/NDCG measure the embedding model (jina-v2-base-code), not tea-rags-specific
  contribution. The right question is "does adding git signals help, holding
  embedding constant?" — that's an A/B ablation, not an absolute benchmark.

Core methodology: A/B ablation with identical embedding model on both arms.

  ARM A (control):    same embedding + tea-rags hybrid without git signals
  ARM B (treatment):  same embedding + tea-rags hybrid + git preset

  Δ(B - A) = pure git enrichment contribution, decoupled from embedding choice

This is a MORE HONEST and MORE PRODUCT-RELEVANT positioning:
  "We don't replace your embedding model. We add measurable git-aware reranking
   on top of any embedding model, with delta proven across real task categories."

Approach: 4 ablation experiments in GitEnrichmentAblationBench (tea-rags-mcp-35hr),
plus refined SWE-bench ablation (tea-rags-mcp-pdpe), all with natural ground truth
from git/CVE (no LLM-as-judge, no human labeling needed):

  1. Bug debugging       → bug-hunt preset      → SWE-bench Lite ground truth
  2. Tech debt audit     → techDebt preset      → git-derived ground truth
  3. Knowledge silos     → ownership preset     → blame-derived ground truth
  4. Security audit      → securityAudit preset → CVE database ground truth

Why P3 (not P0-P2):
- Not blocking any user-facing feature
- ~6-8 weeks of focused work, ~$500-1000 API costs total
- Highest ROI is industry visibility / career, not product correctness
- Do this after core product stabilizes, before public AI-tooling moves

Removed from original scope:
- CoIR adapter (closed as superseded — tea-rags-mcp-cvat)
- LLM-as-judge labeling for GitRankBench (replaced by natural git/CVE ground truth)
- Paper-track timeline for own benchmark (becomes main approve, not future research)
- Comparison vs multiple embedding models (irrelevant — embedding is held constant)

Preserved scope:
- SWE-bench Lite, but refocused on Recall@K ablation (not headline % resolved)
- Reproducibility kit (Docker, scripts, raw outputs) — tea-rags-mcp-lof0
- Publication: blog post + arXiv + HN — tea-rags-mcp-iy14

Cross-cutting requirements:
- Pre-registered hypotheses BEFORE first run (anti-cherry-picking, dated GitHub issue)
- Bootstrap 95% CI on every reported number
- Wilcoxon signed-rank for paired comparisons (A vs B on same task)
- Per-signal ablation (drop one signal at a time within each preset)
- Open repo with Dockerfile + raw outputs as Release artifacts
- Compare ARM A vs ARM B only — no inter-system comparison needed (decoupled)

Acceptance for the epic to close:
- All 4 ablation experiments published with raw numbers + reproducibility kit
- SWE-bench Lite ablation published with Recall@K-as-headline framing
- One blog post with the headline: "Git enrichment adds +X-Y% on real tasks,
  independent of embedding model — measurements, reproducibility, code"
- arXiv preprint draft for GitEnrichmentAblationBench (submission, not acceptance)

title: TAB-completion for --project <name> in CLI
description: Yargs completion: complete --project values from CollectionRegistry.list() names. Both bash and zsh.
close_reason: Landed in commit 4dd22f13 — yargs .completion() wired to listProjectNames(); covers --project and projects unregister/info --name, skipped for projects register --name.

title: Multi-query search: accept query as array in semantic_search and hybrid_search
description:
## Idea

Allow search endpoints (semantic_search, hybrid_search, find_similar) to accept query as an array of strings instead of a single string.

```json
{
  "query": ["first variant", "second variant"],
  "limit": 10
}
```

## Why

- Query expansion / HyDE: agent rephrases user intent into N variants, sends one call, gets fused results
- Reduces N round-trips (N tool calls → 1 tool call)
- Server-side fusion is cheaper than client-side merge (Qdrant Query API supports prefetch + RRF/DBSF fusion natively since 1.10)

## Scope

- Schema: query: z.union([z.string(), z.array(z.string()).min(1)])
- Embedding: batch-embed all variants in one Ollama/ONNX call (already supported by EmbeddingProvider)
- Qdrant: use Query API prefetch with one branch per variant, fuse with RRF on server
- Hybrid: each variant gets dense+sparse pair, fused together
- Single-string case unchanged (backwards compatible)

## Out of scope

- Auto-expansion on server (agent supplies variants explicitly)
- Per-variant weights (equal weight via RRF)

title: Verify optimizer error recovery via collection update (1.18)
description:
Qdrant 1.18 fixes #8767: when a collection update triggers optimizer recreation, optimizer errors are now cleared — collection can recover without daemon restart.

For tea-rags this means: if embedded Qdrant gets into a degraded state due to an optimizer crash, we can fix it via a no-op collection update instead of asking the user to kill the daemon.

Acceptance:
- Add a recovery path in src/core/adapters/qdrant/ or IngestFacade: detectAndClearOptimizerErrors()
- Triggered when: get_index_status detects degraded state with optimizer error
- Action: issue a minimal collection update (e.g. update collection params with current values) → confirm error cleared
- Surface via tea-rags prime digest infra-health section if recovery happened
- Cover with a unit test that mocks degraded state + recovery

Smaller scope: could also be just a docs note 'if Qdrant is degraded, run X CLI command to recover' without code. Decide during impl.

title: Benchmark ARM SIMD cosine perf on Apple Silicon (1.18)
description:
Qdrant 1.18 ships ARM SIMD optimization for cosine normalization (#8640). Most tea-rags users run on Apple Silicon M1-M4. Document the win so we can include it in release notes.

Acceptance:
- Run a representative hybrid_search workload on tea-rags codebase before (1.17) and after (1.18) on M-series Mac
- Capture: avg latency, p95 latency, throughput
- Add a short note to docs/website/ or CHANGELOG entry quoting the numbers
- If gains are <5%, just note 'free improvement, no measurable change for tea-rags workload' — don't oversell

No code changes needed in tea-rags. This is purely measurement + comms.

title: Enable prevent_unoptimized=true for bulk initial indexing
description:
Qdrant 1.17.1 added deferred point updates via prevent_unoptimized=true. Skips immediate optimizer triggering on each upsert batch — runs optimization once at the end. Big win for the cold-start indexing flow where we upsert tens of thousands of chunks in sequence.

Implementation sketch:
- Add a flag to IngestFacade.indexCodebase() / pipeline path: bulkMode=true
- When bulkMode, pass prevent_unoptimized=true to qdrant upsert calls
- After last batch, call /collections/{name}/optimize or upsert with prevent_unoptimized=false to flush
- For incremental reindex (reindex_changes), keep current behavior — optimizer should run inline

Measure: before/after wall-clock for indexing a known-size repo (e.g. tea-rags itself). Expect 15-30% faster first index on large projects.

title: Skill: per-cluster SKILL.md generation
description:
## Goal

Skill that generates one `SKILL.md` per detected cluster, so the agent gets
targeted context when working inside a specific functional area of the
codebase. Replaces GitNexus `gitnexus analyze --skills` flag.

## Lives in

`.claude-plugin/tea-rags/skills/generate-cluster-skills/SKILL.md`.

Output goes to `.claude/skills/generated/<cluster-name>.md` in the target repo.

## Depends on

- Cluster detection from Slice 6 (`tea-rags-mcp-uxsr`) — **hard dependency**,
  this skill is a no-op without clusters.
- Existing MCP tools: `get_clusters`, `get_cluster_members`, `find_symbol`,
  `hybrid_search`.

## Per-cluster SKILL.md template

```markdown
---
name: working-in-<cluster-name>
description: Use when modifying code in <cluster-name> — covers <key responsibilities>
---

# Working in <cluster-name>

## Files in this cluster (<size>)

- <relPath 1> — <one-line role>
- <relPath 2> — <one-line role>
...

## Entry points

<find_symbol output for public exports in cluster>

## Common operations

<hybrid_search top-3 patterns within cluster path>

## Cross-cluster connections

<get_callers / get_callees for entries to other clusters>

## Risk overlay

<top-3 hotspots in cluster from techDebt preset>

## When to invoke

- User working in <relPath pattern>
- User mentions <semantic keywords from cluster summary>
```

## Algorithm

```
1. clusters = get_clusters()
2. for cluster in clusters where size >= MIN_CLUSTER_SIZE:
   a. members = get_cluster_members(cluster.id)
   b. entries = find_symbol(role="public") within members
   c. patterns = hybrid_search(query="common operation", pathPattern=members)
   d. cross_links = get_callers/callees for entries → other clusters
   e. hotspots = hybrid_search(rerank="techDebt", pathPattern=members, limit=3)
   f. write .claude/skills/generated/<cluster>.md
3. Update .claude/skills/generated/README.md with cluster index
```

## Auto-trigger description (critical)

The `description` frontmatter field controls when the agent picks up the
skill. Must be **specific enough** that Claude routes to it on relevant
prompts:

- Bad: "Skill for cluster X"
- Good: "Use when modifying authentication, session handling, or password
  hashing — covers auth/, middleware/auth.ts, and session-store.ts"

Description generation = LLM summarization over cluster's hybrid_search top
results. Quality of clustering and summarization directly drives skill
trigger accuracy.

## Refresh strategy

- Regenerate when cluster membership shifts beyond a threshold (e.g., >20%
  files moved between clusters since last run).
- Otherwise reuse cached SKILL.md.

## Non-goals

- Editing existing hand-written SKILL.md files (only `.claude/skills/generated/`).
- Cross-repo skills (single repo per run).
- Multi-cluster skills (one cluster = one skill, even if related).

title: Skill: codebase-wiki generation
description:
## Goal

Skill that auto-generates a codebase wiki (per-module Markdown + Mermaid diagrams)
from existing tea-rags MCP tools. Replaces the GitNexus `gitnexus wiki` feature
without adding code to the tea-rags MCP itself.

## Lives in

`.claude-plugin/tea-rags/skills/generate-codebase-wiki/SKILL.md` (or wherever
plugin skills are organized).

## Depends on

- Cluster detection from Slice 6 (`tea-rags-mcp-uxsr`) for module boundaries.
- Falls back to top-level directory structure when clusters unavailable.
- Existing MCP tools: `hybrid_search`, `find_symbol`, `get_callers`,
  `get_callees` (Slice 1).
- `claude-mermaid:mermaid-diagrams` skill for diagram rendering.

## Output structure

```
docs/architecture/
  README.md                  # high-level overview, list of modules with cohesion
  <module-1>.md              # per-module page
  <module-2>.md
  ...
```

## Per-module page template

1. **Responsibility** — one-paragraph summary derived from `hybrid_search`
   over the module's path.
2. **Entry points** — `find_symbol` for exported defaults / public APIs.
3. **Key collaborators** — `get_callers` / `get_callees` on top-3 entry
   points to surface cross-module dependencies.
4. **Risk overlay** — top-5 risky paths through the module (from Slice 6
   `find_risky_paths` filtered by cluster).
5. **Mermaid diagram** — call graph of entry points + their first-hop
   dependents, generated via `claude-mermaid` skill.
6. **Navigation hints** — `relPath` links into source for each symbol.

## Algorithm

```
1. Get module list:
   - get_clusters() if available
   - else: top-level dirs as pseudo-modules
2. For each module:
   a. relevance_summary = hybrid_search(query="main responsibility", pathPattern=module/**)
   b. entries = find_symbol(role="export-default" OR "public") in module
   c. for each entry in entries[:3]:
        callers = get_callers(entry.symbolId)
        callees = get_callees(entry.symbolId)
   d. risky = find_risky_paths(entryPoint=entries[0]) [if Slice 6 done]
   e. diagram = mermaid_save(graph from a-d)
   f. write docs/architecture/<module>.md
3. Generate docs/architecture/README.md with module index and cohesion table.
```

## Non-goals

- Live updates — wiki regenerates only on user invocation.
- Custom themes / branding.
- Multi-language wiki output (only English/Russian as project default).

## Open questions

- Where does the skill output go for monorepos vs polyrepos?
- LLM model used for module summarization — agent's current model or pinned
  small model for cost?
- Update strategy: full regen vs diff-aware (only touch modules changed
  since last run)?

title: Multi-repo / cross-service search and contracts
description:
## Goal

Support querying and reasoning across **multiple repositories** in one tea-rags
instance — covering microservice architectures and meta-monorepos where
services live in separate git repos but call each other over HTTP/gRPC/queues.

This is the one class of task GitNexus solves that tea-rags currently has no
roadmap for. Filed to make it explicit and avoid losing the intent.

## Scope (high-level)

### 1. Multi-collection registry
- Today: each `index_codebase` call creates its own Qdrant collection per project path.
- Needed: explicit registry mapping `repoName → collection` with metadata
  (language stack, last indexed, contract version), similar to GitNexus
  `~/.gitnexus/registry.json`.

### 2. Cross-collection search aggregator
- New MCP tool `search_across(repos: string[], query: string, ...)`.
- Federates `semantic_search` / `hybrid_search` over multiple collections,
  merges by score with cross-collection RRF or normalized similarity.

### 3. Service contract extraction
- New trajectory `ServiceContractsTrajectory` (or sub-trajectory of codegraph)
  that extracts REST/gRPC/GraphQL schemas from code:
  - REST: route registration patterns (Express, FastAPI, NestJS decorators).
  - gRPC: `.proto` files + service definitions.
  - GraphQL: SDL files + resolvers.
  - Async: queue topic names, event schemas (Avro/JSON-Schema).
- Persists in DuckDB `cg_contracts_*` tables (matches `cg_<subtype>_*` convention).

### 4. Cross-repo edge resolution
- A REST call `fetch('/api/users/...')` in service A becomes an edge to the
  matching route in service B (when both are registered in the same group).
- Implementation idea: per-repo extracts `inbound` (routes it serves) and
  `outbound` (calls it makes); a matcher pass joins outbound→inbound by URL
  pattern + HTTP method.

### 5. Group concept
- New `RepoGroup` entity grouping related repos (e.g., `hr-platform =
  [hr-api, hr-worker, hr-frontend]`).
- New MCP tools mirroring GitNexus group concept:
  - `group_create(name)`, `group_add(name, repo)`, `group_remove(name, repo)`
  - `group_query(name, query)` — search across all repos in a group.
  - `group_contracts(name)` — list extracted contracts and cross-links.
  - `group_status(name)` — staleness check per repo in the group.

### 6. Cross-repo trajectory paths (depends on Slice 6)
- Once cross-repo edges exist, `find_risky_paths` can trace from an HTTP
  handler in service A through a network call to service B's handler all the
  way to its DB layer. This is the killer use case.

## Non-goals

- Auto-detection of which repos belong together — explicit `group_create` only.
- Real-time service mesh integration (no live trace ingest).
- Auth/authz across repos (assume single user, single Qdrant instance).

## Open questions

- Storage: extend existing `GraphDbClient` or separate `ContractsDbClient`?
- Naming: align with codegraph (`cg_contracts_*`) or separate namespace
  (`mr_*` for multi-repo)?
- Where do `group_*` tools live in the facade hierarchy — new `GroupFacade` or
  extend `GraphFacade`?
- How does drift detection handle group membership (one repo reindexed but
  others stale → contract matches break)?
- Connection-pool strategy for serving multiple collections concurrently
  (mirror GitNexus's "max 5 lazy-loaded with 5min idle eviction"?).

## Why a separate epic (not part of codegraph)

- Codegraph is per-repo. This is **across** repos.
- Different storage shape (contracts ≠ AST symbols).
- Different MCP surface (`group_*` family vs `cg_*` family).
- Can ship independently of codegraph Slices 1-6.

## References

- GitNexus group commands: https://github.com/abhigyanpatwari/GitNexus
  (`group_sync`, `group_query`, `group_contracts`).
- Codegraph epic: tea-rags-mcp-l26 (parent for shared graph infrastructure
  if reused).

title: enrichment: backfiller.test.ts coverage for failure paths
description: backfiller.test.ts currently covers only happy-path + empty-map. Missing tests: (1) provider.buildFileSignals rejecting (try/catch in backfiller.ts:62-72), (2) ctx.effectiveRoot === null early return, (3) buildFileSignals returns Map without missed path (partial-success branch), (4) buildChunkSignals rejecting in chunk backfill phase (backfiller.ts:155-160), (5) qdrant.batchSetPayload rejecting (silent try/catch). All these error paths exist for a reason — adding tests prevents silent regressions. From code-review of commit b0bfabd6.

title: enrichment: replace base.ts awaitCompletion with light empty-chunkmap path
description: base.ts:218 (post Task 1 of EnrichmentCoordinator split) calls awaitCompletion for the empty-chunkmap early-return path. awaitCompletion now does prefetch drain + backfill scrolls + recovery countUnenriched (~500ms + 2N Qdrant queries) on what was previously sub-ms fire-and-forget. Behavior is correct (markers fully transition completed instead of leaving chunk=pending), but heavyweight on a rare path. Fix: add markEmptyChunkmap(coll, providerKeys) helper to MarkerStore that writes file=completed, chunk=completed for each provider, swap base.ts to call it. From code-review of commit f6cacb2e.

title: Configurable shard_number for collections (parallel shard recovery)
description:
Currently collections are created with shard_number=1 (Qdrant default). Recovery on cold start is per-shard serial → one CPU is used regardless of OPTIMIZER_CPU_BUDGET / MAX_OPTIMIZATION_THREADS (those control runtime/optimization pools, not startup recovery).

Problem: large collections take minutes to recover on cold start (blocks HTTP bind until all shards done).

Proposal:
1. Add shard_number option to index config (env QDRANT_SHARD_NUMBER, default 1 for back-compat; 2-4 reasonable)
2. Pass through createCollection() call when creating a new code_XXXX_vN collection
3. Document tradeoff: more shards = faster cold-start recovery + more parallel indexing, but higher per-query overhead

Context: discovered during multi-core daemon tuning (commit 342cc3f). Env vars work at runtime but startup recovery is shard-bound.

description: Verify existing finalizeProcessing -> startChunkEnrichment flow works correctly now that streaming filters enriched files. Plan says no code changes needed. Chunk 4.

title: Documentation gaps: security, migration, deployment recipes, integrations
description:
## Overview

High-level review of website/docs identified structural gaps after the recent fill-empty-stubs wave. Full structure is in place (Introduction, Quickstart, Architecture, API, Config, Usage, Agent Integration, Operations, Extending, Knowledge Base, RFC, Roadmap, Changelog). Remaining gaps are user-facing sections that serve explicitly-mentioned audiences.

## Gaps (ordered by priority)

### Critical

1. **Security & Privacy page** — "Who It's For" in the landing page explicitly targets "privacy-sensitive teams (finance, healthcare, defense)", but no page answers:
   - What data crosses network boundaries (cloud embeddings API vs Ollama local vs ONNX local)
   - Where data is stored (~/.tea-rags/, Qdrant storage, daemon state)
   - Threat model (API key leakage, Qdrant dashboard exposure on 0.0.0.0)
   - Data residency for OpenAI / Cohere / Voyage (regions, retention)
   - How to audit what leaves the machine

2. **Migration / Upgrading guide** — no page between changelog and operations. Users see BREAKING CHANGE: in commits but nothing consolidated on "how to upgrade from 1.x to 2.x" with sequential steps, pre-flight checks, rollback.

### Important

3. **Non-Claude MCP clients** — website/docs/quickstart/installation.md mentions "other MCP clients" in a single line. Need walkthroughs per client: Cursor, Continue, Zed, Windsurf. Each has its own MCP config format and quirks.

4. **Deployment scenarios / Cookbook** — scenarios are scattered across config/:
   - CI with cached index
   - Air-gapped deployment (Ollama + embedded Qdrant, no internet after install)
   - Team-shared remote Qdrant cluster
   - Monorepo with git submodules (enrichment scope, ignore rules)
   Consolidate into Cookbook or Deployment Scenarios section.

5. **Observability / Production operations** — website/docs/operations/performance-diagnostics.md covers solo-user diagnostics. Missing: metrics to export, logs to ingest (pino once it lands — see tea-rags-mcp-rwom), when to alert, health-check endpoints, SLO guidance.

### Useful

6. **Contributing guide mirror** — CONTRIBUTING.md lives only at repo root. website/docs/extending/development-setup.md is 39 lines — thin. Commit conventions, PR process, TDD expectations, test-pattern rules not surfaced in website docs.

7. **Benchmarks / Performance numbers page** — figures ("~8M LoC on Apple M3 Pro", "1M vectors ~= 500k-1M LoC", "~100ms chunk-level churn with 10 workers") scattered across provider pages and architecture/overview. Consolidate into one Benchmarks page with methodology + hardware baseline + table of measured numbers.

8. **Stability / API versioning policy** — which parts of the MCP schema are stable vs evolving. Which payload fields are guaranteed. Useful for integrators building on top of TeaRAGs.

### Optional

9. **FAQ (conceptual)** — separate from troubleshooting-and-error-codes FAQ (which is operational). "Why not X alternative?", "Offline mode?", "IDE support beyond Claude Code?", "Does it work on Windows?"

10. **Per-skill reference pages** — currently all 6 user-invocable skills live on /usage/skills/index.md (207 lines). Could split into /usage/skills/explore, /usage/skills/bug-hunt etc. for deeper reference. Current form works — split is optional quality-of-life.

## Recommended order

Critical first: 1 (Security) -> 2 (Migration). Then 3 (non-Claude clients, high adoption impact) -> 4 (Deployment recipes) -> 5 (Production ops). Rest on demand.

## Non-goals for this epic

- Not revisiting RFC 0001 / 0002 / 0004 stubs (out of scope per earlier direction)
- Not filling minor-scope gaps (individual preset docs, individual filter field docs — already covered via MCP resources)

## References

- Main docs: https://artk0de.github.io/TeaRAGs-MCP/
- Gap analysis done 2026-04-21

description:
Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-31-ollama-fallback-operation-lock-design.md

Implement OperationLock in src/core/adapters/embeddings/ollama/operation-lock.ts to prevent repeated primary/fallback flapping during long operations (indexing, reindexing). Current usingFallback logic in ollama.ts does not hold the chosen endpoint for the duration of an operation, causing peaks-and-valleys on flaky networks.

Acceptance:

title: Ruby DSL body groups as named symbols in outline

This makes them visible in find_symbol outline and individually searchable. Ruby class-body-chunker already has group.type — use it to build symbolId = ParentClass.groupType. For split groups append index: User.validations1, User.validations2.

title: Rust symbolId: use # separator for &self methods
description: Rust has real instance/static distinction: &self/&mut self = instance method, no self = associated function. Use User#method vs User.new convention like Ruby/Java. Detection: check first parameter for self/&self/&mut self in tree-sitter AST. Prerequisite: Rust needs childChunkTypes for impl blocks to extract methods as separate chunks.

title: TearEggs: negative signal detection for harmful eggs (research)
description:
## Problem
TrueEggs (viable) легко детектить: egg + commit + cosine. Но negative signal (egg навредило) — фундаментально сложнее: отсутствие события не событие, откат может быть по другой причине, DON'T eggs имеют перевёрнутую логику cosine.

## Фундаментальная асимметрия
- **TrueEgg**: egg → код → коммит → cosine показывает связь. Чёткий сигнал.
- **TearEgg**: egg → код → ... что? Молчание. Откат. Или ничего.

## DO vs DON'T eggs — перевёрнутая логика
Egg "не делай X":
- cosine(egg, diff) высокий → запрет НАРУШЕН (плохо)
- cosine(egg, diff) низкий → запрет соблюдён ИЛИ egg не релевантно. Не различить.

## Three signal sources (ranked by reliability)

### Source 1: Code stability collision (pure math, no LLM)
Tea-rags уже знает стабильность кода через git signals.
```
Egg claims X → code shows Y (collision: resolved_symbol changed/deleted)
+ code git signals: commitCount high + ageDays high + bugFixRate low = STABLE
→ код прав, egg устарело → TearEgg

Egg claims X → code shows Y (collision)
+ code git signals: commitCount=1, ageDays=2 = UNSTABLE
→ амбивалентно, код может быть buggy → skip
```
Formula:
```
collision = find_symbol(egg.imprinting[i]) → not found OR signature changed
stability = f(commitCount, ageDays, bugFixRate, churnVolatility)
if collision AND stability > threshold → TearEgg
if collision AND stability < threshold → ambiguous, skip
```
Properties:
- Все данные уже в tea-rags (git signals + resolved_symbols)
- Автоматический, без агента, без человека, без LLM
- Детектит: "egg описывает код, который стабильно рефакторнули"
- НЕ детектит: "egg дало плохой совет" (нет коллизии с кодом, просто плохое знание)

### Source 2: Transcript rejection detection (zero friction)
Фидбек уже в транскрипции — человек говорит "нет/неправильно/откати".
```
egg retrieved at message N
→ agent used egg in message N+1 (cosine(egg.content, agent_message) > threshold)
→ user correction at message N+2..N+4
→ TearEgg candidate
```
Two levels:
1. Heuristic (no LLM): regex "no", "wrong", "не то", "revert", "откати", "неправильно"
2. LLM (Llama 14B): "Did the user reject this knowledge? yes/no" — ambiguous cases

Properties:
- Zero friction — человек уже дал фидбек естественно
- Post-session analysis via ~/.claude/projects/*/conversations/
- Детектит: "плохой совет" (user explicitly rejects)
- НЕ детектит: тихие ошибки (агент использовал bad egg, user не заметил)

Open questions:
- "нет, переделай" (rejection) vs "нет, я имел в виду другое" (clarification) — как различить?
- Window size: N+2..N+4 или шире?

### Source 3: Agent collision detection (orchestration)
При retrieval tea-rags сравнивает egg с текущим кодом. Если коллизия — добавляет warning в response:
```
"⚠️ Collision: egg says TwilioClient.send, code has SmsSender.deliver. Code stability: high."
```
Агент вынужден отреагировать → реакция в транскрипции → Source 2 анализирует.

Properties:
- Forcing mechanism для агента (collision warning нельзя игнорировать)
- Комбинация Source 1 + Source 2: математика детектит, транскрипция подтверждает
- Детектит: subtle wrongness (egg частично устарело, но не полностью)

## Prerequisite
- V1 TrueEggs (kds5) должен работать и накопить статистику
- Живой nest с реальными eggs и retrieval history
- Статистика покажет: нужен ли negative signal или viable + ice age достаточно

## Decision
Research task. Не имплементировать до накопления данных из V1.
Приоритет sources при имплементации: Source 1 → Source 2 → Source 3.

## Related
- EggVolution epic (tea-rags-mcp-e98j)
- Depends on: kds5 (TrueEggs V1 must launch first)
- Depends on: 6315 (imprinting / resolved_symbols for collision detection)
- Existing git signals infrastructure (commitCount, ageDays, bugFixRate, churnVolatility)
- Transcript access: ~/.claude/projects/*/conversations/

title: Curator: bullet pruning, merging, dedup and constitution linting
description:
## Problem
Memory collection grows over time. Without curation: duplicates accumulate, harmful bullets persist, orphaned bullets waste retrieval slots.

## Solution
Curator — periodic maintenance pass over memory collection. Combines ACE-style pruning with Context7 cross-reference validation.

## Curator operations

### 1. Semantic deduplication
- For each bullet pair: cosine(bullet_a, bullet_b)
- If cosine > 0.95 → merge candidate
- Merge rules by source priority:
  - human + human → warn user, never auto-merge
  - human + auto → keep human, transfer counters from auto (helpful+=, harmful+=)
  - human + code_comment → keep both (different lifecycle rules)
  - auto + auto → merge, sum counters, keep higher-quality content
  - auto + code_comment → keep code_comment (AST-linked = more reliable)
  - code_comment + code_comment → merge if same parent symbol, else keep both

### 2. Pruning by quality
- Auto bullets: `harmful > helpful * 2 AND (helpful + harmful) >= 5` → delete
  - Min 5 interactions to avoid pruning on noise (2 harmful, 0 helpful = don't prune yet)
- Code_comment bullets: don't prune by counters — prune only when comment deleted from code
- Human bullets: NEVER auto-prune. If harmful >> helpful → flag for human review, don't delete

### 3. Orphan detection
- resolved_symbols contains symbols not found in code collection → stale
- All resolved_symbols empty + source=auto → delete
- All resolved_symbols empty + source=code_comment → comment was deleted → delete
- All resolved_symbols empty + source=human → warn user: "bullet about X has no code references, still relevant?"

### 4. Constitution linting (from original 3iv6)
Cross-reference validation of static governance (Tier 1):
- All links in CLAUDE.md to .claude/context/*.md → files exist
- All agents in trigger tables → AGENT.md exists
- Code-level claim detection in remaining .claude/ docs:
  find backtick references → find_symbol → exists? signature matches?
  If not → "stale claim, remove or let memory handle this"

## Trigger options
- **Manual**: skill `/curator` — run on demand
- **Post-reindex hook**: after reindex_changes, if >N bullets affected → curator pass
- **Threshold-based**: if collection size > M bullets → trigger dedup + prune

## Curator does NOT:
- Create new bullets (that's reflector / bootstrap)
- Update resolved_symbols (that's auto-synonym resolution at reindex)
- Evaluate helpful/harmful (that's retrieval tracking)
- Summarize or rewrite bullet content (bullets are atomic, not refineable)

## Metrics output
After each curator run, report:
- Bullets merged (count, examples)
- Bullets pruned (count, reasons)
- Orphans detected (count, flagged for human)
- Constitution lint issues (broken refs, stale claims)

## Related
- ACE7 epic (tea-rags-mcp-e98j)
- Depends on: e0tf (schema), rpzq (bootstrap creates bullets), 6315 (resolved_symbols)
- Absorbs original la0g (drift detection) — orphan detection covers this
notes:
## Ice age: adaptive, not calendar-based (2026-03-31 insight)
NEVER use fixed 30-day threshold. Egg freezes only if:
1. Its territory is ACTIVE (commits exist in territory's pathPattern)
2. AND egg is not retrieved during that active period

If territory is dormant (0 commits) → eggs hibernate, no aging.
Territory activity from existing git signals (commitCount by pathPattern).

This prevents killing valid eggs just because developer didn't touch that area for a month.

Motivation: user wants ownership analysis as a separate concern from risk-assessment.

title: Multi-reranking: server-side multi-preset rank with overlap detection
description: Single MCP call that runs rank_chunks with N presets simultaneously and returns results cross-referenced by overlap count (tier: critical/high/medium). Eliminates 4 separate round-trips + client-side merge in risk-assessment skill. Key decisions: merge in RankModule, overlay union strategy, scoring within tier, pagination of merged results.

title: Index payload field 'name' for fast leaf-symbol lookup
description:
## Problem

The 'name' payload field (leaf identifier of a chunk's AST symbol, e.g. 'processChunk' for method ChunkerService.processChunk) is written to every chunk but has NO Qdrant index. Today find_symbol works around this by matching against 'symbolId' (full path, text-indexed) — which forces substring-y matching against 'ChunkerService.processChunk' instead of a direct hit on 'processChunk'.

Currently indexed payload fields: relativePath (keyword+text), language/fileExtension/chunkType (keyword), symbolId (text), parentSymbolId (text), git.file.enrichedAt/git.chunk.enrichedAt (datetime). 'name' is missing.

## Scope

1. Add Qdrant payload index on 'name': **keyword + text** (whitespace tokenizer, NO stemmer/phrase/stop-words — identifiers are not natural language).
2. Extend find_symbol to prefer 'name' match for leaf-only queries (input.symbol has no dot), fall back to symbolId for qualified queries (input.symbol contains dot).
3. Schema migration v14: add the index on existing collections; cold-path (SchemaManager#initializeSchema) also creates it for new ones.

## Why NOT stemming/phrase/stop-words (original task scope)

Initial framing assumed Qdrant 1.15+ stemming would help symbol search. It doesn't:
- Stemming 'Handler' -> 'handle' merges class Handler with method handle() and variable 'handling' — noise, not signal.
- Phrase matching on symbolId is meaningless (symbolId is a dot-separated path, not a phrase).
- Stop-words ('is', 'for', 'it') are part of real identifiers (isReady, forEach, itemList) — disabling them silently breaks search.

Stemming/phrase belong on **natural-language payload fields** (future: commit messages from tea-rags-mcp-8xbc, separated docComment, headingPath.text). Defer that to a separate task when those fields actually exist as searchable.

## Affected files

- src/core/adapters/qdrant/client.ts (createPayloadIndex — already supports keyword+text, no signature change)
- src/core/adapters/qdrant/schema-manager.ts:initializeSchema (cold path)
- src/core/infra/migration/schema_migrations/schema-v14-name-index.ts (new)
- src/core/infra/migration/schema_migrations/index.ts (register v14)
- src/core/domains/explore/strategies/symbol.ts:buildSymbolFilter (route to 'name' for leaf queries)
- tests/

## Out of scope

- Stemming / phrase matching / stop-words (deferred until NL payload fields exist)
- Renaming 'name' -> 'symbolName' (separate task, breaking payload change)
- Indexing parentName/parentType/imports[]/headingPath.text
close_reason: Won't fix. Original premise (stemming for find_symbol) is misframed: identifier stemming creates noise (Handler/handling/handle merge), phrase matching is meaningless on dot-separated symbolId, stop-words break real identifiers (isReady, forEach). Existing symbolId text index already handles leaf-lookup correctly via default 'word' tokenizer (splits on dots). For NL-field stemming, defer to tea-rags-mcp-8xbc (Git history indexing with commit messages) — that's where stemming has clear ROI and the createPayloadIndex extension pattern (text-params, migration, MCP filter schema) can be designed once and reused for headingPath.text later if a concrete user story emerges.

title: Upgrade ESLint 9.x → 10.x
description:
Risk: LOW. Already on flat config (eslint.config.js), which is the main v10 requirement.

Breaking changes:
- Legacy .eslintrc removed — we already use flat config, OK
- Config lookup starts from file dir, not cwd — single project, OK
- FlatESLint/LegacyESLint API removed — we don't use programmatic API
- Node.js >=20.19 || >=22.13 || >=24 — we're on 24, OK

Approach:
1. npm install eslint@10 @eslint/js@10
2. Verify typescript-eslint@8.57 compat (added ESLint 10 support in 8.56)
3. Run npx eslint src/ — compare warnings/errors with current baseline (2 existing warnings in explore-facade.ts:443)
4. Run full test suite

title: Semantic batch changes: search-driven codemod engine
description:
Batch code transformations powered by semantic search + AST rewriting. Killer feature vs Sourcegraph: semantic find (not regex) + structural replace (not text).

Components needed:
1. Transform engine — codemod DSL describing what to change
2. AST rewriter — applies transformation to tree-sitter nodes
3. Diff generator — preview before applying
4. Batch runner — applies across N repos, generates PRs

Integration path: comby (language-agnostic structural matching) or jscodeshift (JS/TS).

Flow: hybrid_search finds locations by meaning → tree-sitter parses AST → transform engine rewrites → diff preview → batch apply across repos.

Key advantage over Sourcegraph: step 1 is semantic with git context (finds deprecated patterns, tech debt, ownership-aware), not regex.

title: Adaptive batch size: auto-reduce on GPU pressure
description: Start with bs=16 in daemon. If inference latency exceeds 2x baseline average, automatically drop to bs=8. Track rolling average of last 10 batches. This allows larger GPUs to benefit from bigger batches while protecting against memory pressure on smaller GPUs. Requires timing instrumentation in daemon handleEmbed.
close_reason: All 8 tasks implemented: constants, BatchSizeController, worker probe+timing, daemon integration, client exposure, pipeline propagation, paths, tests. 2185 tests pass.

title: Optimize ONNX embedding performance
description:
ONNX embedding performance optimization epic.

## Done
- ✅ Warm-up batch — first batch latency 828→415ms (-46%)
- ✅ ONNX session options — graphOptimizationLevel=all, enableGraphCapture, GELU approx, denormals
- ✅ Daemon-side batching — client sends bs=32, daemon splits to GPU-safe bs=8 internally
- ✅ Pipeline batch size — raised from 8 to 32 (4x fewer socket roundtrips)

## Tested & Rejected
- ❌ executionMode=parallel — neutral on WebGPU (no parallel ops for single model)
- ❌ preferredOutputLocation=gpu-buffer — incompatible with pipeline (pooling/normalize on CPU)
- ❌ freeDimensionOverrides — neutral on WebGPU (JIT already optimizes after first call)

## Remaining
- In-process daemon (tea-rags-mcp-eg0) — zero socket overhead for single-session
- Adaptive batch size — start bs=16, auto-reduce on GPU pressure
- Tokenizer caching — verify tokenizer reuse between batches

## Benchmarks
- Baseline (pre-optimization): ~130s for 1520 chunks
- Current (all optimizations): ~115s (-12%)
- First batch cold start: 828→415ms
notes: Subtasks: tea-rags-mcp-eg0 (in-process daemon), tea-rags-mcp-ql3 (adaptive batch), tea-rags-mcp-57m (tokenizer caching)

title: Squash-aware session normalization for git signals
description:
Replace raw commitCount with sessionCount in git trajectory signals. Agent-style development produces burst commits (10-15 per feature) that inflate churn/stability/bugFixRate signals.

## Core idea
Group commits into sessions by (author + time gap). Gap threshold ~30min (configurable). One burst of agent commits = 1 session.

## Implementation
1. Add groupIntoSessions() in git reader — group by author + gap threshold
2. New fields in GitFileSignals: sessionCount, fixSessionCount  
3. Derived signals read sessionCount instead of commitCount for churn/stability/burstActivity
4. bugFixRate uses fixSessionCount/sessionCount (fix sessions = sessions starting with fix: commit)
5. Requires reindexing

## Signals affected
- churn, stability, burstActivity, volatility — use sessionCount
- bugFixRate — use fixSessionCount/sessionCount (post-gap fixes = real bugs)
- ownership — group by (author + gap), preserves multi-author info

## Notes
- Works for both solo and team modes (teams also use agents with burst commits)
- Large teams squash to main anyway, but feature branches still benefit
- Session grouping key: author + gap, not just gap (prevents cross-author merging)
- Priority P3: improvement, not blocking anything

title: Composite debtScore interaction signal (age × churn)
description:
## Context
Approach C from techDebt redesign brainstorming (2026-02-25).
Deferred — linear model with L3 blending covers the use case adequately.

## Problem
Linear scoring: age + churn means code ranks high if EITHER old OR churny.
True debt = old AND churning (multiplicative relationship).

## Proposed solution
Add `debtIndex = normalize(age) × normalize(churn)` as a new composite signal.
This requires extending the scoring pipeline to support derived signals
(currently all signals are computed independently in calculateSignals).

## Design options
1. Add `debtIndex` as a regular signal computed in calculateSignals from other signals
2. Add a second-pass scoring layer that computes composites from first-pass signals
3. Support arbitrary signal expressions in preset config (overkill)

## Deliverables
- Design doc for composite signal architecture
- Implementation with TDD
- Update techDebt preset to include debtIndex

## References
- docs/plans/2026-02-25-techdebt-preset-redesign.md (Section 3.5)
- docs/researches/chunk-metric-corrections.md

title: Fix duplicate pipeline log files per session
description:
Multiple pipeline log files created per single reindex session.

## Symptom
3 log files created within 1 second:
- pipeline-...-39-403Z.log (992 bytes, header only)
- pipeline-...-39-588Z.log (992 bytes, header only)
- pipeline-...-40-474Z.log (27KB, actual data)

## Root Cause
DebugLogger is instantiated at module scope:
  export const pipelineLog = new DebugLogger();
Constructor creates log file immediately if DEBUG=1.

Module is imported from 3 places:
- src/code/indexer.ts
- src/code/pipeline/chunk-pipeline.ts
- src/code/pipeline/index.ts (re-export)

If ESM resolves these as separate module instances (different import paths or specifier quirks), each creates its own log file. Only one instance actually receives pipeline events.

## Fix Options
1. Lazy init: create log file on first write, not in constructor
2. Singleton with deduplication: check if log file already exists for current session
3. Merge outputs at shutdown: collect all log paths and concatenate at session end

Option 1 (lazy init) is the cleanest — no file created until first actual log entry.

title: Add C++ language support (chunking + codegraph + resolution)
description:
Codegraph+chunking for C++: classes, structs, templates, namespaces, methods, operator overloads, virtual/inheritance; preprocessor macros. HARDEST — templates + overload resolution + macros; expect resolver precision tradeoffs.

Standard add-language checklist (per .claude/rules/domains-language.md + add-language-hook skill):
1. tree-sitter grammar dep (tree-sitter-<lang>) in package.json + worker in-thread import.
2. domains/language/<lang>/ provider: LanguageFactory.create() native switch case (NOT a registry lookup).
3. chunking/ hooks: bodyChunker, commentCapture, headerExtractor + symbolId convention (symbolid-convention.md).
4. walker/name-of.ts: which AST nodes become cg_symbols NODES (symbol-node coverage — see audit pi2p for the parity bar to hit).
5. resolver/: scoped CallResolver (fq_name + scope) so call edges resolve, not name-match. File import edges too.
6. codegraph config registration (tree-sitter-<lang> in CodegraphEnrichmentProvider parser list).
7. tests: preserve examples, validate it/describe counts; live force_reindex smoke on a real sample repo.
Scope = breadth gap (language absent entirely), NOT within-language node gaps. Reference: code-graph-rag-mcp supports this language; tea-rags currently has no parser/walker for it.

title: codegraph: LSP post-pass for unresolved method-call edges
description:
## Why this issue exists

Current codegraph resolution is purely syntactic — tree-sitter walker emits
calls, per-language resolver (TSCallResolver, JavascriptCallResolver,
RubyCallResolver, etc.) tries to pin a target using import paths,
localBindings, classFieldTypes, and known higher-order patterns. Anything
beyond this surface — inferred types, generic instantiation, type narrowing
through control flow, return-type chains without explicit annotations,
external `.d.ts` declarations — leaves the call with `target_symbol_id =
null`.

The Codegraph Follow-ups Quality epic (`tea-rags-mcp-4j5i`) tracks
incremental improvements to the syntactic resolver (`wbj3` dynamic
dispatch, `j8yr` type-annotation reading). Even fully implemented those
cap at ~0.92-0.94 resolveSuccessRate — the rest needs real type checking.

LSP servers (typescript-language-server, pyright, sorbet, gopls,
rust-analyzer) ALREADY exist and ALREADY answer
`textDocument/definition` with full-fidelity type inference. We can
exploit them as a POST-PASS for whatever the syntactic resolver couldn't
pin, without putting an LSP on the hot path of every call.

## Architecture

```
Pass 1 (today, tree-sitter walker):  extract calls (file, line, col, receiver)
Pass 2 (today, syntactic resolver):  resolve what's possible
                                     → ~3349 edges, ~6000 unresolved
Pass 3 (NEW, LSP post-pass):         for each unresolved call:
                                       lsp.textDocument/definition({uri, line, col})
                                       → location → resolve to symbol_id
                                       → write additional edge
```

LSP server is:
- Spawned LAZILY per indexed project on first post-pass
- Cached per (collectionName, language) — reused across files
- Subject to idle timeout (default 5 min) — shut down to free RAM
- Out-of-process — never touches tea-rags daemon heap
- Optional — if LSP binary not installed, skip pass 3 with warning

## Detection / configuration

`bootstrap/config/lsp.ts` (new): auto-detects per-language LSP availability:

```
typescript / javascript: prefer typescript-language-server, fallback to tsserver
python: prefer pyright (`pyright-langserver`), fallback to pylsp
ruby: prefer sorbet (`srb tc --lsp`), fallback to solargraph
go: gopls
rust: rust-analyzer
java: jdtls
```

Path resolution: `which typescript-language-server` etc. Doctor command
gains an "LSP post-pass: typescript-language-server FOUND / NOT FOUND"
section. Operators can install missing LSPs at their pace; missing LSPs
just disable post-pass for that language (degrade to syntactic-only).

Config knob:
- `CODEGRAPH_LSP_POSTPASS: "auto" | "off"` (default "auto" once shipped)
- `CODEGRAPH_LSP_TIMEOUT_MS: 10000` per definition query
- `CODEGRAPH_LSP_IDLE_MS: 300000` (5 min) before shutting the LSP

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New `infra/lsp/lsp-client.ts` — minimal LSP client subset:
  `initialize`, `textDocument/didOpen`, `textDocument/definition`,
  `shutdown`. Use existing `vscode-jsonrpc` npm package.
- [ ] New `infra/lsp/lsp-pool.ts` — per-project LSP server cache, lazy
  spawn, idle eviction (mirror codegraph daemon's pool idle pattern).
- [ ] New `domains/trajectory/codegraph/symbols/post-resolver.ts` — runs
  AFTER the streaming resolver finish, scrolls unresolved edges from
  `cg_symbols_edges_method`, batches them per file, queries LSP, writes
  resolved targets back via UPDATE.
- [ ] Hook in CompletionRunner step between file finalize and chunk
  enrichment: file edges resolved → LSP fills in residuals → chunk
  enrichment sees fuller graph for fanIn/fanOut.
- [ ] Per-language LSP detection in doctor:
  `tea-rags doctor --lsp` shows which LSPs are wired vs missing.
- [ ] Failure modes:
  - LSP not installed: log once, skip post-pass for that language.
  - LSP crashes mid-query: kill process, retry on next post-pass.
  - LSP timeout per query (10s): mark unresolved, continue.
- [ ] Doctor integration test: spawn tsserver in CI, verify that calls
  unresolved by syntactic resolver become resolved after post-pass
  (test fixture: `const arr = getItems(); arr.map(this.transform);`
  where `getItems` returns `Foo[]`).
- [ ] Live validation on tea-rags-worktree: resolveSuccessRate after
  post-pass should reach 0.95+ (vs current 0.345).
- [ ] New stat surfaced in pipeline-log: `byProvider.codegraph.symbols.{
  lspResolved, lspTimedOut, lspSkipped }`.

## Out of scope

- LSP for FIRST-PASS resolution (would be slow + duplicate the existing
  syntactic resolver work). Post-pass only.
- File-level codegraph edges (this issue is method-level). File edges
  rely on imports[] which the syntactic resolver already handles well.
- Custom LSP protocol extensions — stay to spec subset that all major
  LSPs implement.
- Installing LSP binaries via tea-rags. Doctor REPORTS missing, user
  installs via their own channels (npm / pip / gem / etc.).

## Relationship to other Quality issues

This issue, if shipped first, REDUCES the priority of:
- `wbj3` (callback-arg specialization) — LSP handles natively via
  type checker. wbj3 still gives sub-second wins for known patterns
  WITHOUT an LSP installed, so not redundant for offline / minimal-deps
  scenarios.
- `j8yr` (annotation reading + return-type chains) — LSP gives full
  inference, not just syntactic annotations. j8yr still valuable as
  the offline fallback that doesn't require LSP binaries.

`ykj7` (denominator filter) is INDEPENDENT — even with LSP at 95%,
denominator must exclude externals for the metric to mean anything.

## References

- Tree-sitter walker extraction: `chunker/extraction/*-walker.ts`
- Syntactic resolver: `domains/language/<lang>/resolver/*-resolver.ts`
- Edge write target: `cg_symbols_edges_method`
- LSP spec: https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/
- vscode-jsonrpc: npm package used by major editor implementations
notes:
Cost estimate per language (2026-05-29 sizing, senior dev days):

Shared infrastructure (one-time, amortized across all languages):
  LSP client subset (vscode-jsonrpc): 3-5 days
  LSP pool + idle eviction: 1-2 days
  Doctor LSP detection framework: 1 day
  Subtotal shared: ~7 days

Per-language incremental (after shared infra):
  TypeScript (tsserver mature, no bundle pain): 3-4 days + 1-2 days/year
  Python (pyright clean, env via venv straightforward): 4-5 days + 2-3/year
  Go (gopls cleanest, modules first-class): 3-4 days + 1-2/year
  Rust (rust-analyzer mature): 3-4 days + 1-2/year
  Ruby (bundle activation + rbenv/asdf + LSP fragmentation): 7-9 days + 2-3/year
  Java (jdtls heavy config, multi-module Maven/Gradle): 8-10 days + 3-4/year

Ruby specifically — what makes it expensive vs other languages:
1. Bundle activation: LSP must run in `bundle exec` context for gem
   resolution. Non-trivial detection of bundler binary, Gemfile location,
   rbenv/asdf Ruby version switching per project. +2-3 days unique to Ruby.
2. LSP fragmentation: 3 viable options (Ruby LSP / Solargraph / Sorbet),
   each with different capability matrix, install path, version coupling.
   Need a Ruby-specific adapter that tries Ruby LSP first, falls back to
   Solargraph, skips Sorbet (low adoption). +1 day.
3. Rails magic: ActiveRecord generates methods (User.find_by_email).
   Solargraph needs solargraph-rails gem; Ruby LSP has nascent Rails
   support; Sorbet needs sorbet-rails introspection. tea-rags doctor
   must surface "Rails detected, install solargraph-rails for full
   coverage". Documentation overhead.
4. Solargraph cold start (5-15s for medium projects) — user-visible
   latency on first post-pass query. Need progress reporting.

Coverage estimate for Ruby specifically:
- Current syntactic resolver: ~0.20-0.25 (Ruby weakest due to
  Zeitwerk autoload, method_missing, blocks, Rails magic)
- After ykj7 (denominator filter): ~0.35-0.45
- After Ruby LSP post-pass: ~0.70-0.85 (LSP doesn't fully cover Rails
  dynamic dispatch either, but covers method_missing chains, blocks,
  and standard Ruby idioms)

Recommended language order (cheapest first, biggest absolute gain last):
  1. TypeScript (10-12d total, proves architecture, biggest user base)
  2. Python (+4-5d incremental)
  3. Go (+3-4d incremental)
  4. Ruby (+7-9d incremental, biggest absolute resolveSuccessRate gain)
  5. Rust (+3-4d incremental)
  6. Java (+8-10d incremental, biggest config burden)

Total for full coverage: ~38-45 days senior dev. Ships per language
per release. Tea-rags daemon must handle "some languages have LSP, some
don't" cleanly — degrade gracefully.

Skip-cost: leaving Ruby out means Ruby projects stay at ~0.20-0.25
resolveSuccessRate — visibly worse than TS/Python users see. Acceptable
short-term, painful long-term given Ruby is a primary indexed language
(taxdome, sinatra, huginn, production-rails-app).

title: Runtime adaptive sizing of the enrichment worker pool
description: Follow-up to the unified enrichment worker pool (spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-27-unified-enrichment-worker-pool-design.md). The initial design sizes the shared enrichment WorkerPool from a static config knob (ingest.tune.enrichmentPoolSize, default max(1, cores/2)). This task: make the pool size adapt at RUNTIME instead of a fixed start value — e.g. grow/shrink worker count based on observed embedding-loop pressure, queue depth, per-batch enrichment latency, or available CPU headroom, so heavy reindexes get more enrichment threads while light/idle periods release them. Should respect the codegraph affinity invariant (1 worker per active collection holds shared run-state — never migrate a pinned collection mid-run) and the embedding HTTP loop's need for headroom (avoid oversubscription that re-introduces the 'ямы'). Investigate availableParallelism, queue-depth signals from WorkerPool, and the existing chunkerPoolSize for a consistent adaptive strategy across both pools.

description: Tier 3. Add community channel link (Discord or Telegram) for feedback + community building. Lowers barrier for early-user feedback loop.

title: [epic] Codegraph Slice 12 (speculative): Additional sub-graphs catalogue (inheritance, test, security, runtime, docs)
description:
⚠️ ДОЛЖНО БЫТЬ ОГОВОРЕНО ПЕРЕД РЕАЛИЗАЦИЕЙ
⚠️ МОЖЕТ БЫТЬ ОСОЗНАННО ПРОПУЩЕН

Speculative. No spec authored, no concrete user demand at creation time. Owner-only decision to activate. Each candidate sub-graph below would be a SEPARATE epic when activated — this is the parking spot, not a single-deliverable slice.

The codegraph architecture (cg_<subtype>_* naming + createCodegraphTrajectories() factory + L2 trajectory pattern) supports N additional sub-graphs without contract changes. Slice 5 (temporal) validates this; future sub-graphs land in their own slice numbers when picked up.

**Catalogue of candidate sub-graphs (ranked by intuition only — not by demand):**

1. **cg_inheritance_*** — type hierarchy graph (extends/implements edges per language)
   - Use: 'Show all subclasses of UserBase' / refactoring base-class methods
   - Distinct from call graph: capturing IS-A, not USES
   - Per-language: TS extends/implements, Python MRO, Ruby class hierarchy, Go embedding, Rust trait impl

2. **cg_test_*** — test → source mapping
   - Use: 'What test covers UserService#authenticate?' / coverage gap detection
   - Sources: chunk.chunkType='test' already exists in Slice 1 payload; need explicit edges from test chunk to tested chunk
   - Heuristic: test file naming (user.test.ts → user.ts), then refine via call edges (test calls X → X is tested)

3. **cg_security_*** — taint annotations / vulnerability tags
   - Subset of dataflow (Slice 8) with security focus
   - Per-language source/sink registries (Express req.body → sql query, etc.)
   - Could be standalone if full dataflow is too heavy; standalone version captures only known sources + known sinks + direct path

4. **cg_runtime_*** — runtime tracing integration
   - Use: 'Hot paths' (high-frequency calls) vs 'cold dead code' separation
   - Sources: OpenTelemetry traces, Pyroscope profiles, custom tracer feeds
   - Edges: weighted by call frequency / latency
   - Requires external infra (tracer must run); offline analysis pulls trace data

5. **cg_documentation_*** — doc-to-code edges
   - Use: 'This README example references X.method — keep them in sync'
   - Sources: code blocks in markdown, doc-comment cross-refs, OpenAPI examples
   - Integration with existing doc indexing (markdown chunks already have parentSymbolId for headings)

**Before activating ANY of the above, confirm:**
1. The specific sub-graph has a concrete user / use case (not just 'cool to have')
2. Storage cost analysis vs existing sub-graphs (some, like cg_runtime_*, can be larger than symbols)
3. Update cadence — temporal updates per commit, runtime updates per trace batch, etc.
4. Whether it deserves its own L2 trajectory or fits as derived signal in existing one
5. Spec authored before opening the worktree
